sentences.text: Not-- at higher-- is not anymore.
Because of the poverty, and the government is-- this I'm saying with finesse-- is corrupted.
And they don't think about the people.
They think who going anywhere, how much more it could grow.
And for example, a worker gets $200 a month.
For this, still he have to pay some taxes in it.
So it is-- but it's a beautiful country.
It's bigger than the States.
Only it's missing the management.
It's like Russia used to be.
Like now, I should say Russia is-- and the people are nice people.
They are not anti-Semite.
They're really nice people, and good natured people.
Not only-- not-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
Milton Belfer.
B-E-L-F-E-R.
Meier Belfer.
No.
The 24th of August, 1922.
I was born in Lodz, Poland.
The street was [POLISH] Number 2.
Was a-- a big textile city.
We had about 300,000 population, over 300,000, which was more than one third of Jewish population.
More than one third German.
And less than one third Polish.
Yeah.
It was a textile industry which call in the world as the second Manchester.
Yes.
The most of them.
Most of them was in the textile business.
We were-- the family was my father, my mother, and five children.
I am the youngest.
We was-- we was living, of course, in a Jewish neighborhood.
My family was religious.
I should say a very religious family.
My father had a beard.
All the children had payots.
I went-- I'm a youngster, I went to a Shiva after the cheder.
And the tradition was strictly Jewish tradition.
No.
We had been an-- a-- a big building which we lived also in this building.
The building belonged to our parents.
And we had a very comfortable five room apartment before the war.
Jacob.
Or I should say Yankel in Yiddish.
Yankel Belfer.
My mother was Sarah Belfer.
I had another-- the oldest was a sister which called Zelda.
She was married.
I was very young, I think four-- three, four years old.
She was the oldest sister.
I have another brother which his name was Mordechai.
Another brother.
The second brother was Israel.
The third brother was Shulom.
And me, my name was Meier.
He took care on the building.
And we had also a textile store, and in textile store my father only help.
The main was my mother, which she-- we had sales-- sales people.
And my-- the most of it, my mother took care of it.
Yes.
To learn.
If he had a chance, he took out at home.
He-- we was living near-- it was a [YIDDISH].
And in this [YIDDISH], in afternoon, the people get together and to learn some [YIDDISH], things like this.
And he loved it.
We have a strictly religious kosher home.
And the custom was Friday we always had an [YIDDISH].
This mean people, poor people, which they came [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from outside the city.
And they did not have it spend the Shabbos.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
So they got back from the synagogue.
And our street was two houses away.
He know it already, which people could afford it.
So we get always for Shabbos, we call it a [YIDDISH].
Poor people should come and eat.
Oh.
Who prepared the food for them?
We had a Jewish girl which watched for kosher.
We have a Polish girl which was the maid, I should say.
[POLISH] was the maid.
But a Jewish lady, girl, I should say, she watched the kosher.
Of course.
Was a very Alexandria steeple.
Next the building was it.
No.
This was a small synagogue, about three, four [YIDDISH].
But was a-- a synagogue-- I should say two minutes from our house.
And was a big, big [YIDDISH].
Lodz was a very Jewish type of school, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] where we lived was a special Jewish neighborhood.
The-- the-- the janitor was a goy.
The rest around in this neighborhood was not living goyim.
Please.
For the Shabbat.
Yes.
The baking.
My mother make it Tuesday.
And in our house was a bakery.
The people, the girls, one of the girls took it to the bakery to bake it in the bakery.
We had our-- we-- we also had a special big stove with six-- how do I say-- cook places.
It was divided.
Was special one side for milk and the other side for flesh.
Was a strictly religious home.
Yes.
She was a good cook, but she only prepared it, I should say.
But I had a sister which she was the oldest, before she married she always helped-- I should say, to me I called her Mother Zelda.
I was the youngest.
This is what they say when I was small.
But she took care on the household before she married.
Yeah.
No.
Not close by.
I had-- the grandparents are from a little town which called Peshischa.
And Mother was related to one of the biggest rabbi in the world, which call Simcha Bunim from Peshischa.
He was a very big known rabbi, which he died long time ago.
And as I-- when it was [YIDDISH], the-- I should say the people would belong to this sector went for [YIDDISH].
So I remember every year, they took our [INAUDIBLE], the Hasidim.
Went to [INAUDIBLE], and this was far related of my mother.
But he was a very known call it [YIDDISH] in this time.
What do you remember about the holidays?
What was Passover like?
Very strictly.
We did not eat bread.
Was very strictly.
And the matzahs have to-- when we-- I remember when we ball the matzahs, when we made it, they stopped and they make a new-- I should say prepared.
And my mother, my father watch-- I was a kid.
I was the youngest at home.
And I remember was special [YIDDISH], my father should see this is kosher.
Of course.
We have seders at home.
Of course.
And the whole family was.
It was [YIDDISH], I would say.
It was strictly obeyed, the holidays and the Shabbat in our house.
I-- go ahead.
Was also a holiday, for-- all the holidays was for us a holiday in the tradition of the Jewish religion.
And it was-- we had [YIDDISH] people.
We had, I would say, a [YIDDISH].
[YIDDISH].
We were-- I would say dinner.
Special dinner.
And came people this time, since we live in a very strictly Jewish-- come with the mask to make and a little form they should get something, a few cent.
This, the most of them children with.
But we accepted very much.
It was [YIDDISH].
No.
Very seldom.
I didn't come to it, very seldom.
Hanukkah was also celebrated, but not so-- I should say Hanukkah is not a [YIDDISH] with the-- which you don't need to work.
Hanukkah.
So was observed, the candle lights, the most of it, should be prepared.
And we make every day a special food which we call it latkes.
That was [YIDDISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's made me a little-- to remind.
I went to a-- a Jewish school.
It was a private school, I should say.
It was school from the government, but I-- we-- all the children went in a private school.
And when I finished school, I went to a shiva.
Short time, but there were-- all the rest of were there.
Finished semicha.
This mean they went out of the shiva.
They got certificates to be able to-- the oldest only, he did it.
He was very religious till the war.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS]
Mordechai.
This was the oldest brother.
All of us wear payots.
So very religious family.
And we had a very traditional Jewish home.
Shabbos was Shabbat.
And no work.
You know, now work and more playing.
You have to rest or learning something.
For instance, I have to say what I repeat the whole week, which Daf or from the Gemara or [YIDDISH].
To see this, I learned something.
Yes.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Older brother did it the same way.
But I don't remember, as I was the youngest at home.
I would say in our neighborhood, a big percentage was religious Jews.
Where we used to live.
We had-- in Lodz, we had over 150,000 Jews in the city of Lodz.
Of course, not all was-- but the stores, very little stores was opened Shabbat.
In our neighborhood, didn't happen should Shabbat be open a store.
Never.
But heart more to the center of the city, it happened.
I didn't know exactly who were.
But in our neighborhood was the percentage of Jewish was very high, and only the Gentiles was only the janitor.
In-- no.
I didn't had.
Till the war-- I should say almost till the war.
I didn't have friendly friends.
I mean friends to go out.
Only Jewish.
Yes.
Yes, we had-- my parents had business deals [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with the Christian Germans because in the textile mill.
In France, also, Germans which-- I should say maybe they were not so-- I know them.
I was the youngest at home.
They don't look to me as to be a Nazi.
They're-- sometimes out father tell in the ghetto repeat this.
We didn't know the different.
They know that we are religious Jews.
My father had a beard.
This-- we didn't see anti-Semitism by them.
Yes, of course.
We were-- maybe for this Lodz, our city, was more textile, a working city.
And the people would live for many years, for generation, could be there was not so much anti-Semitism.
In mine opinion-- personally, this-- I think the Polacks was more anti-Semitic than the Germans.
In my time.
I'm talking about mine home town, Lodz.
I don't recall it, except to go to pray, which I had obligation.
I was the youngest at home.
I started a little hobby and the war got-- to playing football.
Which was not allowed by mine father.
So I did it some day, came home from cheder.
Next to our house was a place, as children, played a little football.
But special should go in-- in a team to play.
No, it was by children.
Yes.
This--
I-- I-- it happened this, the Polish saved in little towns, I should say, some Jews.
It's true.
Not I-- I didn't say all.
As I remember, a lot of people left Europe from Poland more because of the anti-Semitism in Poland.
Now, Germany.
Till Hitler came to the power, as we know, Jews was living very comfortable.
And till Hitler came to the power.
Yes.
It happened.
But I should recall it immediately.
I know it happened, anti-Semitism before the war.
I should say, before Hitler.
I don't know.
I wouldn't say, because [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was not in this time involved in it.
No.
Not college.
I should be a rabbi or-- or study and later be a businessman.
But I should first get [YIDDISH].
To be a rabbi.
Really, as-- as all my brother-- no, two brothers-- had [YIDDISH], and other two, we was not so to it.
And the war will stop.
The other-- we-- the younger two, no.
Not as I recall it.
Because I remember a-- a-- a proper religious.
This was told to me in the family in the war time.
This-- we have a big house.
And my mother was anxious to sell the house, big building, and go and live in Israel.
This was between family.
I heard it only from my brother, which he died after the war.
We were left, this-- my father said, Israel is not still religious people should go there.
And he didn't want to sell.
And he died.
Of course, we need to have a strong Israel in [INAUDIBLE].
No.
In the-- before the war, I didn't had any knowledge of it, in this.
I had to school and school, in the later years, shiva.
And it was not-- it was a different, this-- I as the youngest at home should came up and give-- tell my oldest brothers and my father and mother what they should do.
[LAUGHS] It was a different generation.
Of course.
Yeah.
I had.
It was not like now.
Was more a religious type.
We invited friends for kiddush.
Was not like now.
For kiddush, I would say was a dinner, big dinner, family.
And we gave in the-- a kiddush in the-- by the rabbi.
We prayed by rabbi.
And I was called to the sefer.
It was not like now.
Was different.
Was on the small scale.
No.
The party was when it was the dinner in our house, only for family.
Not for our family.
For the whole family after we come from the kiddush, how we say in the synagogue.
Was synagogue [YIDDISH] where the rabbi had a special room, which we prayed there.
And after this, we get together at our home, the family.
Was a big family.
Not only our-- my father had two brothers.
My mother had two sisters, in this little-- in this town.
So it was a party, a family party.
Yes.
We knew it, what had happened.
But really, I-- I didn't believe-- we didn't believe it so hard as-- as it has happened.
Was hard to believe, but we-- we knew it.
No.
No, we didn't thought it will happen.
For one reason.
My father was a very religious man, and as I said before, we had somebody who was interested in buying the building.
And my mother was more Zionist.
We should go to Israel.
Because the rabbi had a daughter there.
The rabbi alone-- this is a big rabbi.
Had a daughter in Israel.
But she lived over in Israel, and my brother saw her after the war.
And we as religious people, we didn't-- the family-- didn't thought-- it was not enough kosher.
This what I understand.
I should say in '38, '38.
Hitler came in '33.
So I should say four years later, we find out this in-- we knew it, what happened.
But we didn't believe it till around in '37, '38.
The-- the communication also was not like now.
We didn't have the television at home.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We didn't have the television at home.
Religious people didn't have in this time televisions.
So we had-- the communication between outside the country was not like today.
